 In addition, of course, all capabilities offered by your coding agent of choice are supported and driven by our ADT MCP Server.

With the introduction of the ADT MCP Server, developers can unlock a truly agentic ABAP development experience. The ADT MCP Server makes ABAP development tools easily accessible to AI systems, streamlining development tasks, simplifying workflows, and improving efficiency.

To learn more about the available MCP tools in ADT for eclipse, see:   Model Context Protocol Tools | SAP Help Portal

Furthermore, step by step, we will achieve a similar level of ABAP AI capabilities support in ADT for Visual Studio Code.

To learn more about the available MCP tools in ADT for Visual Studio Code, see: Model Context Protocol Tools | SAP Help Portal

 

With that, go and explore the re-invented ABAP AI offering!

 Coauthors,

 @karl_kessler @niloofar_naseri

entry.title: Transitioning from SAP Business ByDesign to SAP Cloud ERP
entry.content:
Advancing from SAP Business ByDesign to SAP Cloud ERP — What Growing Companies Should Consider 

SAP Business ByDesign has enabled many midsize organizations to run their businesses with integrated, cloud-based ERP—bringing together finance, HR, procurement, project management, and more in a single system. For years, it has provided a structured, reliable platform that supports operational efficiency and global business models. 

But as businesses evolve, new questions begin to surface: 

  • Can our ERP keep pace with increasing scale and complexity? 
  • How easily can we adopt new innovations like AI, automation, and advanced analytics? 
  • Are we equipped to support new business models or acquisitions? 
  • What will we need to operate as a truly data-driven enterprise? 

For organizations beginning to ask these questions, SAP Cloud ERP becomes an important consideration—not as a reaction to what’s not working, but as a proactive step toward what’s next.

This post explores why SAP Business ByDesign customers evaluate SAP Cloud ERP, what benefits it offers, and how to approach a transition with clarity and confidence.  

Why SAP Business ByDesign Customers Reevaluate Their ERP Strategy?

SAP Business ByDesign was designed as a comprehensive cloud ERP for midsize businesses—and for many, it continues to deliver strong value. However, growth-oriented organizations often reach a point where new demands introduce additional complexity: 

  • Expanding into new markets with more advanced compliance requirements 
  • Managing multi-entity, multi-country operations at greater scale 
  • Supporting more complex supply chains or service delivery models 
  • Integrating with a growing landscape of applications and data sources 
  • Enabling real-time analytics and predictive insights across the business 

At the same time, expectations for enterprise technology are evolving. Capabilities like embedded AI, continuous innovation cycles, and highly intuitive user experiences are no longer differentiators—they are becoming standard. 

As these expectations increase, organizations may begin to assess whether their current ERP environment can fully support their long-term ambitions. 

SAP Cloud ERP enters this conversation as a modern foundation designed for scalability, continuous innovation, and flexibility—helping organizations prepare for the next phase of growth without adding unnecessary complexity. 

 How SAP Cloud ERP Supports the Next Stage of Growth 

For SAP Business ByDesign customers, the key question is not simply what’s different—but what will improve across the business. 

Customers transitioning to SAP Cloud ERP often point to several meaningful advantages: 

  • Continuous Innovation 
    SAP Cloud ERP delivers regular updates with new capabilities, including analytics, automation, and AI. This reduces the need for large-scale upgrades and allows organizations to continuously benefit from innovation. 
  • Scalable Architecture
    As organizations grow in size and complexity, SAP Cloud ERP provides a foundation that can support higher transaction volumes, broader global operations, and more sophisticated business models. 
  • Embedded Analytics and Real-Time Insights 
    SAP Cloud ERP integrates analytics directly into business processes, enabling faster and more informed decision-making without relying on separate tools or delayed reporting cycles. 
  • Automation and Intelligent Processes
    From finance to supply chain, automation reduces manual effort and improves consistency—allowing teams to focus more on strategy and exception management. 
  • Flexibility Through Extensibility 
    Organizations can tailor the system using modern extension frameworks without impacting the core—maintaining stability while enabling differentiation. 
  • Reduced Operational Overhead 
    With SAP managing infrastructure, updates, and system operations, internal IT teams can shift their attention from maintenance to innovation and business enablement. 

For many companies, these benefits result in greater agility—helping them respond faster to changing market conditions and opportunities.  

Addressing Common Questions from SAP Business ByDesign Customers 

Transitioning from SAP Business ByDesign to SAP Cloud ERP is a strategic decision, and organizations typically have several important questions. 

  1. Is transitioning from SAP Business ByDesign complex? 
    A transition requires planning, but it doesn’t need to be disruptive. Many organizations take a phased approach—evaluating current processes, preparing data, and aligning implementation with business priorities. 
  2. What happens to our existing configuration and processes? 
    SAP Business ByDesign systems often reflect years of configuration and optimization. Moving to SAP Cloud ERP is an opportunity to simplify and adopt standardized best practices where possible, while retaining critical differentiators through extensions. 
  3. Will we lose the benefits of a cloud ERP? 
    No—SAP Cloud ERP builds on the advantages of cloud delivery while expanding capabilities in scalability, innovation, and integration. 
  4. How long does the transition take?
    Timelines vary depending on scope, complexity, and readiness. The most successful transitions are guided by business goals rather than predefined timelines. 
  5. Can SAP Cloud ERP integrate with our existing applications?
    Yes. SAP Cloud ERP is designed to integrate seamlessly with SAP and third-party solutions, supporting modern integration standards and architectures.

 What Customers Experience After Transitioning 

Organizations that have transitioned from SAP Business ByDesign to SAP Cloud ERP often describe a shift in how ERP supports their business. 

  • Greater Process Standardization and Scale 
    Core processes across finance, procurement, and operations become more consistent and scalable, making it easier to support growth and expansion. 
  • Improved Visibility and Decision-Making
    Real-time insights across the organization reduce reliance on manual reporting and enable more proactive decision-making. 
  • Less Time Spent on System Maintenance
    With infrastructure and updates handled by SAP, IT teams can focus more on innovation and supporting business initiatives. 
  • Enhanced User Experience 
    Modern, intuitive interfaces improve adoption and usability across business functions. 
  • Stronger Foundation for Innovation 
    Organizations are better positioned to adopt emerging technologies, including AI and advanced analytics, as part of their core ERP environment. 

These outcomes reflect a broader evolution: ERP becomes a platform for continuous improvement, rather than a system that requires periodic transformation. 

 How to Approach the Transition 

Every SAP Business ByDesign customer has a unique starting point, but several best practices can help guide a successful transition: 

  • Assess your current landscape: Understand how SAP Business ByDesign supports your processes, data, and integrations today. SAP has enabled an automatic scope suggestion in its Digital Discovery Assessment (DDA) for the functionality that is in use in your SAP Business One system. This provides a head start in analyzing and deciding on the scope of your SAP Cloud ERP system. Learn more:
    https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/missiondetail/4647/4936/ 
  • Define your future-state vision: Align the transition with your strategic goals, such as expansion, efficiency, or innovation. 
  • Simplify and standardize: Use the transition as an opportunity to reduce complexity and adopt best practices. 
  • Plan in phases: Break the journey into manageable steps that minimize disruption and deliver value early. Know that SAP provides tools to support your transition. For example, Extract, Transform, and Load (ETL) tools help ease the effort required for migrating data into the SAP Cloud ERP system. Learn more: 
    https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/missiondetail/4424/4710/ 
  • Leverage expertise: Engage experienced partners and SAP resources to guide the process and reduce risk. For example, SAP can guide you with a proven transition methodology and best practices. Learn more:
    https://me.sap.com/roadmapviewer/group/658F507A-D6F5-4B78-9EE1-0300C5F1E40F/roadmapcontentpage/46c76488235e4388b843fb30c82882e3 

The goal is not speed—it’s alignment. A well-planned transition ensures that your ERP evolves in step with your business. 

Taking the Next Step 

If you are running SAP Business ByDesign and beginning to think about how to support your next stage of growth, exploring SAP Cloud ERP is a natural next step. 

SAP has created a dedicated transition page that is a great place to get started. See https://www.sap.com/products/erp/grow/transition-for-midmarket.html for additional details. 

A transition doesn’t need to be hasty—but it does benefit from early planning and clear priorities. By understanding your options and defining your path forward, you can ensure your ERP strategy supports both current needs and future ambitions. 

For many organizations, advancing to SAP Cloud ERP is not about replacing what works today—it’s about building a stronger, more flexible foundation for what comes next.

entry.title: Transitioning from SAP Business One to SAP Cloud ERP
entry.content:
Advancing from SAP Business One to SAP Cloud ERP — What Growing Companies Should Consider

SAP Business One has helped thousands of small and midsize businesses establish control, visibility, and efficiency across finance, sales, purchasing, and inventory. For many organizations, it has been a reliable and practical foundation for years. 

But as businesses grow—expanding into new markets, increasing transaction volumes, or diversifying operations—questions begin to emerge: 

  • Can our ERP scale with us? 
  • Are we able to access real-time insights across the business? 
  • How quickly can we adapt to changing requirements?
  • What will it take to support the next phase of growth? 

For companies asking these questions, SAP Cloud ERP becomes increasingly relevant—not as a replacement for what has worked, but as a next step to support what comes next. 

This post explores why SAP Business One customers begin evaluating SAP Cloud ERP, what value it can bring, and how to approach a transition with clarity and confidence.  

Why SAP Business One Customers Reassess Their ERP Landscape 

SAP Business One is designed to be straightforward, efficient, and cost-effective. However, growth-oriented organizations often reach a stage where new demands introduce additional complexity: 

  • Expanding into multiple countries or legal entities 
  • Managing higher transaction volumes and operational scale 
  • Navigating evolving regulatory and reporting requirements
  • Integrating with a growing ecosystem of applications 
  • Delivering faster, more data-driven decisions 

At the same time, expectations around technology are changing. Business users increasingly expect intuitive experiences, embedded analytics, automation, and seamless connectivity across systems. 

As a result, what once felt simple and sufficient can begin to feel limiting—not because SAP Business One is inadequate, but because the business itself has outgrown its original requirements. 

SAP Cloud ERP enters the conversation as a way to support this next phase—offering a modern, scalable foundation designed for continuous change without added complexity.  

How SAP Cloud ERP Supports Growing Businesses 

For organizations evaluating SAP Cloud ERP, the most important question is not “What features does it offer?” but “How will it improve how we operate?” 

Customers transitioning from SAP Business One frequently highlight several key benefits: 

  • Continuous Innovation 
    SAP Cloud ERP delivers regular updates with new functionality, eliminating the need for disruptive upgrade cycles. Businesses can adopt innovations as they become relevant rather than planning large-scale system changes. 
  • Scalable, Standardized Processes 
    SAP Cloud ERP is built on proven best practices that help standardize operations across finance, procurement, sales, and supply chain. At the same time, it allows flexibility where differentiation matters. 
  • Real-Time Visibility 
    Analytics are embedded directly into business processes, enabling faster decision-making with up-to-date, integrated data—rather than relying on separate reporting tools or delayed insights. 
  • Automation and Intelligence
    Routine, manual tasks can be automated, allowing teams to focus on higher-value activities such as analysis, planning, and customer engagement. 
  • Reduced IT Complexity
     Infrastructure, system maintenance, and updates are managed by SAP, reducing the operational burden on internal IT teams and enabling them to focus on innovation instead. 

For many SAP Business One customers, these advantages translate into improved agility—the ability to respond faster to opportunities and challenges as the business evolves.  

Addressing Common Questions from SAP Business One Customers 

Transitioning to a new ERP system is a significant decision. SAP Business One customers typically raise several practical questions when considering SAP Cloud ERP. 

  1. Is transitioning from SAP Business One complex or disruptive? 
    A transition does require planning, but it doesn’t need to be disruptive. Many organizations take a structured approach—reviewing existing processes, cleaning and organizing data, and then implementing in phases aligned with business priorities. 
  2. What happens to our existing processes? 
    SAP Business One systems often reflect years of process refinement. Moving to SAP Cloud ERP presents an opportunity to simplify and standardize where possible. Many existing processes can be mapped to standard cloud best practices, reducing complexity while preserving what works. 
  3. Will we lose flexibility? 
    Not at all. While SAP Cloud ERP emphasizes standardization, it also supports extensions and integrations that allow businesses to maintain differentiation without complicating the core system. 
  4. How long does the transition take? 
    There is no universal timeline. The duration depends on factors such as organizational readiness, data quality, and scope. The most successful transitions align the roadmap with business goals rather than treating it as a purely technical implementation. 
  5. Can SAP Cloud ERP integrate with our other systems? 
    Yes. SAP Cloud ERP is built for integration, making it easier to connect with other SAP solutions, third-party applications, and modern digital platforms. 

 What Customers Experience After Moving to SAP Cloud ERP 

While every journey is different, common outcomes emerge across organizations that have transitioned from SAP Business One to SAP Cloud ERP. 

  • Simplified Core Operations
    Standardized processes across finance and operations reduce complexity and make it easier to scale as the business grows. 
  • Faster, More Informed Decisions 
    Real-time access to data improves visibility across the organization, reducing reliance on manual reporting and enabling quicker responses. 
  • Less Time Spent on Maintenance
    With technical operations managed in the cloud, IT teams can shift focus from system upkeep to supporting business innovation. 
  • Improved User Experience 
    Modern, intuitive interfaces help drive adoption across the organization, improving productivity and engagement. 
  • Greater Readiness for Growth 
    Perhaps most importantly, companies position themselves to expand—with an ERP foundation that can adapt as they evolve. 

These outcomes reflect a broader shift: ERP becomes less about managing transactions and more about enabling business performance.  

How to Approach the Transition 

Every SAP Business One customer starts from a different place, which is why there is no one-size-fits-all path. However, several steps can help set a strong foundation: 

  • Assess your current system: Understand how SAP Business One is being used today—across processes, data, and integrations. SAP has enabled an automatic scope suggestion in its Digital Discovery Assessment (DDA) for the functionality that is in use in your SAP Business One system. This provides a head start in analyzing and deciding on the scope of your SAP Cloud ERP system. Learn more:
     https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/missiondetail/4724/5008/. 
  • Identify growth drivers: Align the ERP transition with your strategic objectives, whether that’s expansion, efficiency, or innovation. 
  • Simplify where possible: Use the transition as an opportunity to reduce complexity and standardize processes. 
  • Plan in phases: Break the journey into manageable steps that minimize disruption while delivering value early. Know that SAP provides tools to support your transition. For example, Extract, Transform, and Load (ETL) tools help ease the effort required for migrating data into the SAP Cloud ERP system. Learn more:
     https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/missiondetail/4639/4928/ 
  • Leverage expertise: Work with experienced partners and SAP resources to guide the process and reduce risk. For example, SAP can guide you with a proven transition methodology and best practices. Learn more:
     https://me.sap.com/roadmapviewer/group/658F507A-D6F5-4B78-9EE1-0300C5F1E40F/roadmapoverviewpage/2669d3902b244345ac1f9586ab433d73. 

The goal is not to move quickly—it’s to move deliberately, with a clear understanding of what success looks like for your organization.  

Taking the Next Step 

If you are currently running SAP Business One and beginning to think about your next stage of growth, exploring SAP Cloud ERP is a natural step. 

SAP has created a dedicated transition page that is a great place to get started. 

A transition doesn’t need to be rushed. But early planning helps ensure that your ERP landscape evolves in step with your business—supporting innovation, scalability, and resilience over the long term. 

For many organizations, advancing to SAP Cloud ERP is not about replacing what works today. It’s about building a stronger foundation for tomorrow.

entry.title: How to: Mass Update of field Incoterms Part 2 in Business Partner Master Data (Public Cloud)
entry.content:
This post describes a simple and repeatable approach to mass‑updating the field Incoterms Part 2 (LFM1-INCO2) in Business Partner Master Data using the new mass apps for business partner master data in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Business Scenario

You need to update Incoterms Part 2 for multiple suppliers in the Business Partner master. Instead of manual changes, SAP provides a structured three-step mass update process with built-in validation and control.

However, there are a few pitfalls that I will point out here, as well as step-by-step guide of how to achieve this mass update.

Step 1: Export Master Data – Business Partners

Fiori App: Export Master Data – Business Partners (F6551A)

  • Select the required fields:
    • Incoterms (Part 2) (LFM1-INCO2_L)
    • Even though you want to change LFM1-INCO2, it is necessary to use LFM1-INCO2_L (see below under pitfalls for an explanation)
  • Filter or include all Business Partner Numbers that need updating
  • Click Export to download the Excel file

 


Step 2: Maintain Data in Excel

  • Update the column Incoterms Part 2 (LFM1-INCO2_L) with the desired values

💡  Ensure that:

  • Only valid values are entered
  • No structural changes are made to the template

 


Step 3: Manage Mass Maintenance – Business Partners

Fiori App: Manage Mass Maintenance – BP (F7974)

  • Create a new Mass Maintenance Process
  • Upload the updated Excel file

 

  • In status “Stopped for Review”, review the proposed changes
  • Confirm the update by selecting “Continue” 

 


 

  • Once the process reaches “Completed”, all changes are applied and can be verified in the business partner master data


Verification of Results

The successful results can be verified in two ways:

  • by navigating directly from within the process using "Show all Changes"

  • by checking in the App Display Supplier List (F1861)

Pitfalls

While performing the process for the first time, I experienced two issues from which I want to prevent you.

1. No Process Template available

If no process template is available in the app Manage Mass Maintenance – BP (F7974), you may encounter the error:

"Change not possible; object does not exist"

Solution:

Activate the required feature toggle:

  • Go to customizing activity: 106573 – Activate Features
  • Enable: MDC_BP_CLOUD_READY
  • Transport this customizing

After activation, the mass maintenance process template will be available.

 

2. Usage of the wrong field

Even though you want to update field LFM1-INCO2, it is necessaty to use Field LFM1-INCO2_L (not LFM1-INCO2).

Having Incoterms Location 1 (LFM1-INCO2_L) in the file will update all required INCO2 data, including the Incoterms (Part 2) as well. 

Using LFM1-INCO2 to update Incoterms (Part 2) would be more logic), however this initially left me with no performed changes.

Summary

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, mass changes to Business Partner master data can be handled efficiently by using a three step approach. SAP supports the process with validation steps throughout the process chain. Before performing the mass update, make sure the prerequisites are given and pay attention to the usage of the correct fields.

References

  • Switch to the new mass apps for business partner m... - SAP Community
  • Switch to the New Mass Maintenance Apps for Business Partner Master Data on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal
  • 3660827 - Process template is missing in the Manage Mass Maintenance-BP app - SAP for Me

Kerisha Lawrence

Public Cloud | IAM, Procurement, Post Go-Live

https://www.linkedin.com/in/kerisha-lawrence

entry.title: SAP Autonomous Enterprise in S/4HANA Transformations
entry.content:
Recently I completed my SAP Certification in Positioning the Autonomous Enterprise, first time I appeared for the exam in new format and for the different topics then our traditional topics like certification production planning or IBP. Thought to share my experience and learning via this blogpost about how the certification can help us during SAP S/4HANA transformation projects. Lets start….

Introduction
The success of an SAP S/4HANA implementation is no longer measured solely by production system go-live, process standardization, or cloud migration. With the changing world towards automation and AI, organizations are increasingly asking a more strategic question, like:

How can we use S/4HANA as a foundation for becoming an autonomous enterprise?

For SAP consultants, this shift creates both an opportunity and a responsibility. Instead of delivering transactional systems that automate predefined workflows, consultants must design intelligent business processes that leverage AI (Artificial Intelligence), semantically rich business data, and autonomous decision-making.

The SAP Autonomous Enterprise vision transforms consultants from system implementers into strategic advisors who help organizations build adaptive, self-optimizing business operations.

What Autonomous Enterprise Means in an S/4HANA Context

In an SAP S/4HANA landscape, the autonomous enterprise is an operating model where business processes can:

  • Detect changes and exceptions in real time
  • Analyze business context using enterprise data
  • Recommend best possible optimal actions
  • Execute approved tasks autonomously (where human interventions are required)
  • Continuously learn and improve outcomes

This capability is enabled by combining:

  • SAP S/4HANA as the digital core
  • SAP Business Data Cloud for trusted business data
  • SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) for integration, extension, and AI
  • Joule as the unified AI interaction layer
  • AI agents and automation services for execution

Rather than adding AI after go-live, consultants should architect autonomous capabilities from the beginning of the transformation journey.

Why Consultants Must Re-think Traditional S/4HANA Implementations

Historically, SAP projects focused on:

  • Migrating legacy processes
  • Replicating customizations
  • Integrating source systems
  • Delivering reports and dashboards

Modern S/4HANA programs must focus on:

  • Process harmonization
  • Clean Core principles
  • Data quality and governance
  • End-to-end process orchestration
  • Al readiness
  • Business outcome realization

Organizations that simply move existing processes to S/4HANA risk missing the full value of the platform. Hence, the consultant's role is evolving from "implementing requirements" to "designing intelligent business capabilities."

Autonomous Enterprise Design Principles for Consultants
Every S/4HANA implementation should be guided by five principles.

  1. Design for Clean Core

Excessive customizations limit an organization’s ability to adopt future innovations, AI services, and autonomous capabilities.

Consultants should:

  • Prioritize standard processes
  • Use SAP best practices
  • Explore the possibilities of EML (Enterprise Management Layer)
  • Build extensions on SAP BTP
  • Minimize custom code within S/AHANA

2. Design Around Business Outcomes

Move beyond functional requirements and define measurable business objectives such as:

  • Reduced planning cycle times
  • Improved forecast accuracy
  • Lower inventory levels
  • Faster financial close
  • Increased order fulfilment rates

3. Treat Data as a Product

Autonomous processes require trusted, harmonized, and semantically rich business data.
Consultants must establish:

  • Master data governance
  • Data ownership
  • Data quality controls
  • Common business semantics

4. Build Human-in-the-Loop Governance

Autonomy does not eliminate human decision-making. Hence, define clear rules for:

  • Automated actions
  • Approval thresholds
  • Exception handling
  • Auditability and compliance

5. Think End-to-End

Autonomous capabilities generate maximum value when processes extend across functional boundaries.

Avoid designing solutions solely around individual modules.

Where to Embed Autonomous Capabilities in S/4HANA Implementations
Here are few examples (not limit to), where we can explore the usability of autonomous capabilities of SAP.
Finance
Traditional implementation focus:

  • General ledger
  • Accounts payable and receivable
  • Asset accounting
  • Financial close

Autonomous use cases:

  • Intelligent invoice matching
  • Cash application automation
  • Predictive cash flow forecasting
  • Financial anomaly detection
  • Automated account reconciliation

    Consultant considerations:
  • Define exception workflows
  • Ensure data quality for predictive models
  • Establish approval governance

Supply Chain

Traditional implementation focus:

  • Production planning
  • Inventory management
  • Procurement
  • Logistics execution

Autonomous use cases:

  • Demand sensing
  • Inventory optimization
  • Automated replenishment
  • Exception-based planning
  • Supply disruption response
  • Scenario recommendation and planning

Consultant considerations:

  • Integrate SAP IBP (Integrated Business Planning)
  • Design cross-functional planning processes
  • Enable real-time visibility

Procurement

Traditional implementation focus:

  • Source-to-pay processes
  • Vendor management
  • Purchase orders

Autonomous use cases:

  • Supplier risk monitoring
  • Automated sourcing recommendations
  • Contract compliance analysis
  • Guided buying experiences

Consultant considerations:

  • Integrate external data sources
  • Define procurement policies
  • Establish supplier governance models

Manufacturing

Traditional implementation focus:

  • Production execution
  • Shop floor integration
  • Quality management

Autonomous use cases:

  • Predictive maintenance
  • Dynamic production scheduling
  • AI based capacity optimization
  • Automated quality inspections
  • Real-time capacity optimization

Consultant considerations:

  • Connect operational technology data
  • Enable closed-loop execution
  • Design event-driven processes

Human Resources

Traditional implementation focus:

  • Core HR
  • Talent management
  • Payroll integration

Autonomous use cases:

  • Skills intelligence
  • Workforce planning
  • Al-assisted recruiting
  • Employee self-service

Consultant considerations:

  • Address privacy requirements
  • Define ethical Al guidelines
  • Ensure regulatory compliance

Key Technology Components Consultants Must Understand
A modern autonomous enterprise architecture typically includes the digital core, data, integration, automation, analytics, and interaction layers required to enable intelligent and scalable business execution.

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud or on-premise
  • SAP Business Technology Platform
  • SAP Business Data Cloud
  • SAP Integration Suite
  • SAP Build Process Automation
  • SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Joule
  • SAP Integrated Business Planning
  • SAP Business Network

Consultants who understand how these components work together are better positioned to design autonomous capabilities early and lead next-generation transformation programs effectively.

Embedding Autonomous Enterprise Across the S/4HANA Project Lifecycle

Discover Phase

  • Identify high-value Al opportunities
  • Define target business outcomes
  • Assess process maturity
  • Evaluate data readiness

Explore Phase

  • Challenge legacy processes
  • Design future-state autonomous workflows
  • Define key performance indicators
  • Prioritize use cases

Realize Phase

  • Configure standard processes
  • Develop BTP extensions
  • Train Al models
  • Integrate business data sources

Deploy Phase

  • Conduct business simulations
  • Validate Al recommendations
  • Train users on Joule and Al agents
  • Establish governance mechanisms

Run Phase

  • Monitor process performance
  • Continuously improve Al models
  • Expand autonomous use cases
  • Measure business value realization

The Future Role of SAP Consultants
As Al capabilities become embedded into enterprise applications, technical configuration skills alone will not be enough.

Successful consultants will combine expertise in:

  • Business processes
  • Industry knowledge
  • Data management
  • Enterprise architecture
  • Al governance
  • Change management

The future belongs to consultants who can answer not only, "How should this process work?” but also, “Which decisions should humans make, and which can be delegated to AI?”

Conclusion

Transformation to SAP S/4HANA is more than a system modernization initiative now. It is the foundation for building an autonomous enterprise.
For consultants, this means shifting from delivering projects to delivering intelligent business outcomes.
The organizations that succeed will be those that design for Clean Core, prioritize trusted data, embed Al into business processes, and establish governance from day one.
The question to SAP consultants is no longer whether to incorporate autonomous capabilities into S/4HANA transformations. The question is how quickly they can develop the skills, methods, and architectures needed to make autonomy a reality.

entry.title: Joule A2A: Propagate the User Identity from Joule into S/4HANA
entry.content:
When AI agents move from proof-of-concept into production, a few qualities stop being optional. Propagating the user identity through the call chain is one of them. With Joule's Pro-Code Extensibility, customers can now plug their own agents into the Joule ecosystem - and the very first question that comes up is: how do I make sure the logged-in user in Joule is the same user that ends up calling the backend API?

This post walks through the end-to-end setup: from the Joule-managed IAS application all the way into S/4HANA Public Cloud, with a custom A2A agent in the middle. The result is an agent that holds no long-lived backend credentials of its own - every backend call is executed against the real, logged-in user's permissions.

Note: With the Joule changes announced at SAPphire 2026, parts of this setup will simplify - especially the upcoming Joule Studio is expected to remove some of the manual steps. The A2A integration itself stays. Treat this post as a snapshot of how it works today, in a customer-managed Joule formation.

Scenario

The setup follows directly from my previous posts: Joule (customer-managed) on a BTP subaccount, plus a custom A2A agent that we built ourselves. If you haven't seen those, start here for the baseline integration and here for the IAS-based authentication of the agent itself.

What's new in this post is what happens inside the agent: instead of calling some toy currency API, the agent calls a real S/4HANA Public Cloud API - the Business Partner OData service - on behalf of the user. Concretely we trigger the standard Businesspartner API in the SAP_COM_0008 communication scenario.

We focus on S/4 Public Cloud here, but the way how to retrieve the users JWT from Joule is relevant for other scenarios as well when you might want to connect to SuccessFactors, S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition and even SAP ECC.

High-level flow

Two token exchanges happen between the user typing a question and S/4 returning data. They look complex on paper but each one has a precise reason.

User in Joule  (logged in via IAS)
│
▼  JWT Bearer App2App exchange
A2A Proxy IAS App
│
▼  JWT Bearer App2App exchange
Custom Agent IAS App
│
▼
Custom A2A Agent (Cloud Foundry)
│
▼  SAML 2.0 Bearer Assertion via BTP Destination Service
S/4HANA Public Cloud  — authorizes against the real user's permissions

The reason for the two-step IAS exchange comes down to one constraint: the Joule IAS application is SAP-managed. We can't add client credentials to it, and we can't add arbitrary dependencies. So we introduce an intermediate A2A Proxy App that we do control, let Joule do the first exchange into it (which it does for us automatically), and then do the second exchange ourselves into the IAS app that represents our agent. Two hops, but each hop sits on an IAS application boundary that we own.

The second part - the SAML Bearer flow into S/4 - is the well-established way of getting from a BTP-issued user token into an S/4 OAuth token. The BTP Destination Service does this for us; we just configure it.

Pre-requisites

  • Joule customer-managed setup
  • IAS tenant
  • BTP subaccount
  • Cloud Foundry deployment
  • S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • SAP_COM_0008 enabled
  • Basic A2A Agent from previous blog

Part 1: IAS Configuration

We need two IAS applications in our IAS tenant. For that we log into the IAS tenant that's trusted by the Joule subaccount. Log into the admin panel of that tenant (typically <tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/admin) and navigate to Applications & Resources → Applications.

A2A Proxy App

This app is the entry point of our A2A app-to-app exchanges. Joule will exchange its token to this app first.

Hit Create, name it A2A_Proxy_App, and choose OpenID Connect.

In addition, expose an API on it - for example a2a_proxy_app_api. The exposed APIs are what other apps' tokens will end up audienced for once they exchange against this app.

Custom Agent App

Now repeat the same with a second application - A2A_Custom_Agent_App. This one represents our actual agent and is the second step of the chain.

Again expose an API on it - e.g. a2a_custom_agent_app_api.

So far nothing crazy. The interesting bit is how they're connected.

Dependencies between the apps

In the IAS world, dependencies define which app-to-app token exchanges are allowed. If app A has a dependency to app B, a token of A can be exchanged for a token of B. We need two dependencies in our chain.

1. Joule → Proxy. On the existing Joule IAS app (usually called das-ias-<subdomain>) we add a dependency to the proxy app. The name must be joule-to-proxy - this exact name is what Joule looks for at runtime.

Pick the previously created A2A_Proxy_App from the dropdown and select its exposed API.

2. Proxy → Custom Agent. Second dependency, this time on the proxy app itself, pointing to the custom agent app. Here the name is your choice - pick something meaningful like proxy-to-target. Remember that name - it has to match a property in the destination configuration we'll create in a minute.

A nice property of this setup: the proxy app is reusable across use cases. The joule-to-proxy dependency is unique per Joule tenant, but for every additional custom agent you add later, you only create a new agent IAS app and a new proxy-to-... dependency on the existing proxy app.

Client credentials for the proxy app

The proxy app's client credentials will be used by the BTP Destination Service to perform the second exchange. On the proxy app, navigate to Client Authentication and add a new secret.

Give it a name and tick all the relevant boxes (OIDC, all token-related options).

You'll get a client ID and secret. Note them down - we need them in the destination next.

Part 2: The Destination from Joule to the Agent

In my previous posts the destination toward the agent used either NoAuthentication or ClientCredentials. This time we use OAuth2JWTBearer, and we add the magic properties that will allow Joule to do the additional token exchange outlined.

Name=BUSINESS_PARTNER_AGENT
Type=HTTP
ProxyType=Internet
Authentication=OAuth2JWTBearer
URL=<your-agent-url>
clientId=<A2A_Proxy_App client id>
clientSecret=<A2A_Proxy_App client secret>
tokenServiceURL=https://<your-ias-tenant>/oauth2/token
tokenServiceURLType=Dedicated

# additional properties
apptoapp=true
tokenService.body.token_format=jwt
x_user_token.jwks_uri=https://<your-ias-tenant>/oauth2/certs
tokenService.body.resource=urn:sap:identity:application:provider:name:proxy-to-target
tokenService.body.client_id=<A2A_Proxy_App client id>
tokenService.addClientCredentialsInBody=false

A few things worth pointing out:

  • The credentials are those of the proxy app, not of the agent app. This destination performs the second exchange (proxy → custom agent). Joule does the first one (Joule → proxy) for us before this destination is even resolved.
  • tokenService.body.resource is what selects the dependency - the value urn:sap:identity:application:provider:name:proxy-to-target references the dependency name we created above. If you named your dependency differently, change it here.
  • x_user_token.jwks_uri tells the destination service where to fetch IAS's public keys to validate the inbound user token. 
  • apptoapp=true is what flips this destination into app-to-app mode in the first place.

The destination is the single most important piece in this setup. Get the additional properties exactly right.

A first end-to-end test

At this point we can already test the IAS+destination setup without writing any new code. Deploy a baseline A2A Agent - for example the Currency Agent from my first blog and make sure to log the context properties in the Agent Executor.

# Log all properties of the context and task objects
logger.info(f"Context properties: {vars(context)}")
logger.info(f"Task properties: {vars(task)}")

These lines would log the user token when asking Joule something. In the agent logs you should see an Authorization: Bearer header arriving with each call.

When you decode that JWT, the payload looks like this:

{
    "ias_apis": ["a2a_custom_agent_app_api"],
    "sub": "P000003",
    "mail": "felix.bartler@sap.com",
    "iss": "https:///",
    "aud": "",
    "scim_id": "be67fc7e-eb03-4422-bc3f-57f0646f090c",
    "azp": "",
    "exp": 1781193148,
    "iat": 1781189548
  }

That's our user identity, signed by IAS, audienced for the custom agent app. The first half of the chain works. You can see the email attribute of my user - this is important because it need to map somehow to the user available in S4 in the end. We even see the specific API on which we created the dependency on. A nice byproduct - because we can use that ias_apis scope now to check if the user is allowed to use our agent at all.

Part 3: S/4HANA Public Cloud configuration for SAML Bearer

Now we turn to the second half: from the custom agent into S/4. We use the OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion flow, which is the recommended way to get from a BTP-issued user token into an S/4 OAuth token. This part follows the official SAP help page very closely - a similar setup also works for SuccessFactors, S/4 Private Cloud, and a number of other systems.

Generate destination trust on BTP

The S/4 system needs to trust the entity that creates the SAML assertion - and that entity is the BTP subaccount's destination service. In your BTP subaccount, navigate to Destinations → Destination Trust and (if there is no SAML IDP trust yet) click Generate Trust. Download the .pem certificate that's generated.

Create a communication user in S/4

In the S/4 app Maintain Communication Users, create a new user. I named mine A2A_CUSTOM_AGENT. The username and password aren't really used at runtime in the SAML Bearer flow, but the comm system requires having a user to bind to and the destination will use them too.

Create a communication system

In Maintain Communication Systems create a new system - again I'm calling mine A2A_CUSTOM_AGENT.

For host, logical system and business system you can put placeholder values (e.g. dummy) - this is an outbound-system definition for our inbound API calls, so the host doesn't matter.

Now the important part: enable OAuth 2.0 Identity Provider and upload the certificate you downloaded from BTP.

Then there's one slightly weird manual step: from the Signing Certificate Subject field copy the CN value (looks like cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/a352a17b-...) and paste it into the Provider Name field.

Finally, under Users for Inbound Communication add the comm user we created. Make sure to add it after activating the OAuth IdP - the S/4 UI is not stateless and the order matters. Add it as Username and Password initially; once you save, S/4 will automatically also register the user for OAuth 2.0 (Confidential Client / SAML 2.0 Bearer Assertion) - which is exactly what we want.

Create a communication arrangement

Communication arrangements bind a comm system to a specific API scenario. In Maintain Communication Arrangements click New and pick scenario SAP_COM_0008 (the Business Partner scenario). I named mine SAP_COM_0008_A2A_CUSTOM_AGENT.

In the new arrangement, link it to the A2A_CUSTOM_AGENT communication system. You can deactivate the replication services - we just need the inbound API.

In the OAuth section, S/4 will show you the token service URL of this arrangement - copy it, we need it in the next step.

Destination from BTP to S/4

The last config piece is a destination on the BTP side. This is the destination our agent will resolve via the destination service - and which produces the S/4 OAuth token via SAML Bearer.

Name=S4_PUBLIC_SAML
Type=HTTP
ProxyType=Internet
Authentication=OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion
URL=https://my30xxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap
Audience=https://my30xxxx.s4hana.cloud.sap        # see note below
Client Key=A2A_CUSTOM_AGENT                       # comm user name
Token Service URL=https://my30xxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/sap/bc/sec/oauth2/token
Token Service User=A2A_CUSTOM_AGENT
Token Service Password=<comm user password>
authnContextClassRef=urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:ac:classes:X509
userIdSource=mail
nameIdFormat=urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:emailAddress

# additional property
x_user_token.jwks_uri=https://<your-ias-tenant>/oauth2/certs

A couple of notes:

  • Audience is your S/4 server URL without the -api part (so my30xxxxx.s4hana.cloud.sap, not my30xxxxx-api...). You can get it from the S/4 UI by clicking your profile picture → Settings → copying the Server field.
  • userIdSource=mail is what tells the destination service to take the mail claim from the inbound IAS JWT and use it as the SAML subject. So for this to work, the user in IAS must have the same email as a (mapped) S/4 user. There are alternatives (e.g. loginName), but mail is the cleanest in most setups.
  • x_user_token.jwks_uri appears here too - and again, without it the destination service falls back to a technical-user assertion and you lose principal propagation.

Dry-run the S/4 Setup

Before we touch any agent code, it's worth verifying the SAML Bearer flow in isolation. The four-steps below is also exactly what our Python code is going to replicate.

Step 1 - get an IAS user token directly (simulating the user login in Joule):

export USER_JWT="$(curl -s -X POST \
  'https:///oauth2/token' \
  --data 'grant_type=password' \
  --data 'client_id=' \
  --data 'client_secret=' \
  --data 'username=user@example.com' \
  --data 'password=...' \
  --data 'token_format=jwt' | jq -r .access_token)"

Here just use your existing Joule User - important - its email has to be present with a user in S4.

Step 2 - get a destination service token (client credentials from the destination service binding):

export DEST_TOKEN="$(curl -s -X POST \
  'https://.authentication..hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token' \
  --user ':' \
  --data 'grant_type=client_credentials' | jq -r .access_token)"

Just a regular token from the destination service retrieved.

Step 3 - resolve the destination, passing the user token - the destination service does the SAML Bearer exchange against S/4:

DEST_RESPONSE=$(curl -s \
  "https://destination-configuration.cfapps..hana.ondemand.com/destination-configuration/v1/destinations/S4_PUBLIC_SAML" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $DEST_TOKEN" \
  -H "X-user-token: $USER_JWT")

export S4_TOKEN=$(echo "$DEST_RESPONSE" | jq -r '.authTokens[0].value')

Here pass again the destination services token. Additionally with X-user-token we pass our original simularted "Joule" Token. You get a opaque token back (so a token without any content - just acting as a key to a session).

Step 4 - call the Business Partner API as the user:

With this token we can now call the BP API as part of the Auth header.

curl -s -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n" \
  "https://my30xxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER/A_BusinessPartner?\$top=2&\$format=json&\$select=BusinessPartner,BusinessPartnerName" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $S4_TOKEN" | jq '.d.results'

If everything is configured correctly, you'll get back business partner records that the user is actually authorized to see in S/4. Their role assignments in S/4 are what's filtering the data here - not anything in BTP, not anything in Joule.

Part 4: The Agent Implementation

Now we tie it all together. The custom agent receives the IAS user token in the inbound A2A request, and we need to forward that token through to the destination service when calling S/4. Surprisingly little code is needed.

Three things change compared to the baseline currency agent from the first blog post:

  1. Pull the user JWT out of the inbound A2A request in the executor.
  2. Thread it down to the tool via LangGraph's RunnableConfig.
  3. In the tool, call the destination service to do the SAML Bearer exchange and then the actual S/4 API call.

Step 1: Extract the user token from the A2A context

The A2A SDK exposes the inbound HTTP headers via context._call_context.state["headers"]. In agent_executor.py, right before kicking off the agent stream:

# Pull the IAS user token out of the A2A RequestContext.
# It comes from the Authorization header that Joule sent to us.
user_jwt = None
try:
    headers = context._call_context.state.get("headers", {})
    auth = headers.get("authorization", "")
    if auth.lower().startswith("bearer "):
        user_jwt = auth[len("bearer "):]
except AttributeError:
    logger.warning("No call_context on RequestContext - running without user token")

async for item in self.agent.stream(query, task.context_id, user_jwt=user_jwt):
    ...

Passing the user_jwt into the agent.stream allows us to use it subsequently.

Step 2: Thread the token to the tool via RunnableConfig

LangGraph has a built-in pattern for this: anything you put under config["configurable"] is reachable in any tool that declares a RunnableConfig parameter.

# agent.py - inside stream()
async def stream(self, query, context_id, user_jwt: str | None = None):
    inputs = {'messages': [('user', query)]}
    config = {
        'configurable': {
            'thread_id': context_id,
            'jwt': user_jwt,   # picked up by every tool that declares config: RunnableConfig
        }
    }
    for item in self.graph.stream(inputs, config, stream_mode='values'):
        ...

And the tool reads it cleanly out of the config - Langchain specifics here on how to pass the variable cleanly to the tool execution:

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableConfig
from langchain_core.tools import tool
from s4_client import call_business_partner_api


@tool
def get_business_partners(
    top: int = 10,
    filter_expr: str | None = None,
    config: RunnableConfig = None,
) -> dict:
    """Query business partners from S/4HANA on behalf of the logged-in user."""
    jwt = (config or {}).get("configurable", {}).get("jwt")
    if not jwt:
        return {"error": "No user JWT in tool context."}
    try:
        return {
            "business_partners": call_business_partner_api(
                jwt, top=top, filter_expr=filter_expr,
            )
        }
    except Exception as e:
        return {"error": f"S/4 call failed: {e}"}

Step 3: The destination service call

Now that we have the token in the tool, we essentially run the same requests we did in the curl dry run now in Python - s4_client.py:

import os
import requests

DEST_SERVICE_URL   = os.environ["DEST_SERVICE_URL"]
DEST_TOKEN_URL     = os.environ["DEST_TOKEN_URL"]
DEST_CLIENT_ID     = os.environ["DEST_CLIENT_ID"]
DEST_CLIENT_SECRET = os.environ["DEST_CLIENT_SECRET"]
DEST_NAME          = os.environ.get("DEST_NAME", "S4_PUBLIC_SAML")


def _get_dest_service_token() -> str:
    """Client-credentials token for the Destination Service."""
    r = requests.post(
        DEST_TOKEN_URL,
        data={"grant_type": "client_credentials"},
        auth=(DEST_CLIENT_ID, DEST_CLIENT_SECRET),
        timeout=10,
    )
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()["access_token"]


def _resolve_destination(user_jwt: str) -> dict:
    """Resolve the destination using X-user-token.

    The Destination Service:
      1. Validates the IAS token (jwks_uri from the additional properties)
      2. Extracts the user from the mail claim (userIdSource=mail)
      3. Generates a SAML assertion with the user identity
      4. Exchanges the SAML assertion for an S/4 OAuth token
    """
    dest_token = _get_dest_service_token()
    r = requests.get(
        f"{DEST_SERVICE_URL}/destination-configuration/v1/destinations/{DEST_NAME}",
        headers={
            "Authorization": f"Bearer {dest_token}",
            "X-user-token": user_jwt,   # drives principal propagation
        },
        timeout=15,
    )
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()


def call_business_partner_api(user_jwt: str, top: int = 10, filter_expr: str | None = None):
    """API call against the S/4 Business Partner endpoint with the propagated user."""
    dest = _resolve_destination(user_jwt)
    auth_tokens = dest.get("authTokens") or []
    if not auth_tokens or auth_tokens[0].get("error"):
        err = auth_tokens[0].get("error") if auth_tokens else "no authTokens returned"
        raise RuntimeError(f"Destination service did not issue a token: {err}")

    s4_token = auth_tokens[0]["value"]
    base_url = dest["destinationConfiguration"]["URL"]

    params = {"$format": "json", "$top": top,
              "$select": "BusinessPartner,BusinessPartnerName"}
    if filter_expr:
        params["$filter"] = filter_expr

    r = requests.get(
        f"{base_url}/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER/A_BusinessPartner",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {s4_token}", "Accept": "application/json"},
        params=params,
        timeout=30,
    )
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json().get("d", {}).get("results", [])

We need the destination service here and can invoke it handing in the X-user-token header. This is the crucial step to trigger the destination service to perform the exchange towards the SAML Bearer Assertion.

The Destination Service then produces a access token that rougly looks like this:

{
  "owner": {
    "SubaccountId": "b5150c......806866e",
    "InstanceId": null
  },
  "destinationConfiguration": {
    "Name": "S4_PUBLIC_SAML",
    "Type": "HTTP",
    "URL": "https://my30xxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap",
    "Authentication": "OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion",
    "ProxyType": "Internet",
    "tokenServiceURL": "https://my30xxxx-api.s4hana.cloud.sap/sap/bc/sec/oauth2/token",
    "audience": "https://my30xxxx.s4hana.cloud.sap",
    "userIdSource": "mail",
    "nameIdFormat": "urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:emailAddress"
  },
  "authTokens": [
    {
      "type": "Bearer",
      "value": "f6c3b8a4d2e44f1a9b7c0d8e2a3b5c6d",
      "http_header": {
        "key": "Authorization",
        "value": "Bearer f6c3b8a4d2e44f1a9b7c0d8e2a3b5c6d"
      },
      "expires_in": "3599",
      "scope": "API_BUSINESS_PARTNER_0001 API_CV_ATTACHMENT_SRV_0001"
    }
  ]
}

Two things worth noting on this response: the token in authTokens[0].value is an opaque S/4 token - not a JWT, you can't decode it. And the scope field reflects the user's roles on the S/4 side - so if the user has API_BUSINESS_PARTNER_0001 here, that's because their S/4 role grants it. If the user is missing on S/4 or the SAML exchange failed, you'd see an error field on the same authTokens entry instead of a value.

That token is now ready to be consumed by S/4.

Note: One non-obvious check is the if not auth_tokens or auth_tokens[0].get("error") line. If the destination service can't validate the user token (e.g. the x_user_token.jwks_uri property isn't set on the destination), it still returns a 200 response - but with an error field instead of a value. Without this check, the code would happily pass an empty string as a Bearer token to S/4, which results in a confusing 401 with an HTML login page in the response body. Don't ask how I know.

The different credentials - for example for the destination service - are passed via the manifest.yaml template visible in the full code example. Or via Service Bindings using VCAP Services env vars in the end.

End-to-End Test

Now finally we can test the setup. We use Cloud Foundry CLI to deploy the custom Agent - use Joule CLI to deploy the Joule capability. Once done we can then just trigger Joule with a sample prompt and see how it has access to the Business Partner Data.

As you can see and also in the logs - the flow works and the users permissions in the backend are reflected. Now your Agent is on step closer for productive use 🙂

Conclusion

The agent itself holds no S/4 credentials. It just forwards the user token it received, and IAS, the destination service and S/4 do the rest. That's the whole point of this setup. Two token exchanges happen along the way, both transparent to the user: first Joule's IAS token is swapped (via the proxy app) into a token for our agent app, then the destination service swaps that token via SAML Bearer for an S/4 OAuth token. The IAS JWT token is passed via the X-user-token header to the destination service and it does some SAP specific magiv to produce the SAML Bearer Assertion that S4 Public can work with. That assertion passes the user context along to S4 as the final authority: if the user has no mapped communication user or their role doesn't grant the API scope, you'll see a 401 from S/4 even though the destination service handed out a token. The picture below summarizes the whole journey end-to-end.

User in Joule  ──▶  Custom Agent  ──▶  Destination Service  ──▶  S/4HANA
   IAS JWT          forwards JWT       SAML Bearer exchange       call as user

This unlocks a lot of follow-up scenarios: the same pattern transfers to S/4 Private Cloud, Ariba, SuccessFactors, ECC - anything that supports SAML 2.0 Bearer Assertion as an inbound auth mechanism. The full code is on GitHub under a2a_principal_propagation/. Make use of the intermediate testing milestones - because this setup is quite lengthy to get right. Hope you enjoyed the content, feel free to leave a comment.

entry.title: Preliminary What’s New Information for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608
entry.content:
Preliminary version of What’s New information and of the Product Assistance are now available for customers and partners to get a preview on the upcoming new and changed features of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608.

You can find this preview on the current SAP Help Portal page SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (see What’s New section “Preview of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608”). To access the Preliminary What’s New information, please use the What’s New Viewer or the Release Assessment and Scope Dependency (RASD) tool.

The RASD tool provides you with a personalized and tailored view on What’s New information based on the activated and used scope within your system. The preliminary information for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 will be available in RASD within the next few days. We recommend using RASD to prepare for upgrades and updates of your system and to focus on the new and changed features that are relevant for you. A valid S-User ID is needed to use the RASD tool and to view all the preliminary content stated above.

For any feedback you may have, try the Commenting Feature for What's New documentation in this release. See this blog for more information.

entry.title: Parallel Line in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: New Implementation Resources
entry.content:
Co-authored by @StefanBarth  and @Nicola_Pace 

In a 3-system landscape (development, test, production), the parallel line offers you a separate workspace in SAP Central Business Configuration and two additional tenants for configuration and testing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. With a separate parallel line, you can develop and test configuration enhancements without affecting the main line.

To help you get the most out of this capability, we’ve assembled a comprehensive set of implementation resources. These complement the product assistance on SAP Help Portal and provide practical, hands-on support whether you're just getting started or looking to optimize your existing setup.

Implementation Resources

The following resources support your parallel line implementation:

SAP Note

The central SAP Note 3754541 provides access to all available SAP Notes that contain release-specific change information for the parallel line. Use this as your starting point to stay informed about the latest updates and corrections relevant to your release.

E-Learning Course

The course Streamlining Parallel Line Adoption in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (approximately 1.5 hours) is available through the SAP Enterprise Support Academy. This training provides practical guidance on implementing and optimizing parallel line functionality in your 3-system landscape, covering:

  • The parallel line concept and its benefits
  • Lifecycle management (branch, rebase, merge)
  • Key actions in SAP Central Business Configuration

Video Resources

Watch quick video tutorials that demonstrate how to work effectively with a parallel line in a 3-system landscape. This curated collection delivers practical, step-by-step visual guidance.

The following videos are available:

  • Rebase Overview – Learn the fundamentals, best practices, and common pitfalls to avoid during the rebasing process
  • Troubleshooting Common Activation Errors:
    • Missing Authorization Issues – Resolve rebase activation errors caused by missing authorizations
    • Incomplete Staging – Learn how to properly close staging as a prerequisite for successful activation
    • Activities Requiring Double Maintenance – Manage errors that occurred during the rebasing process

Each video is concise and focused, allowing you to quickly find the exact guidance you need. Access the full Video Library for Parallel Line on SAP Help Portal today!

FAQ Document

This evolving resource (PDF) offers practical implementation guidance covering a wide range of topics:

  • Eligibility and provisioning
  • Lifecycle management (branch, rebase, merge)
  • Data handling and testing strategies
  • Transport management and extensibility
  • Governance best practices

The FAQ addresses common technical challenges, system restrictions, and integration with SAP Central Business Configuration workflows. It's continuously updated to reflect the latest capabilities and customer insights.

Get Started

All of these resources are designed to work together:

  • Use the SAP Note to stay current on release-specific changes
  • Take the e-learning course to build foundational knowledge
  • Watch the video library for hands-on visual guidance on rebase and troubleshooting
  • Consult the FAQ document for answers to specific implementation questions

Together with the product assistance on SAP Help Portal, you have everything you need to successfully implement and run your parallel line. Start with the resource that matches your current need, and explore the others as your implementation progresses.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition RASD 工具 2026 年新功能：Change History
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 每年发布两个主要版本，每次涉及的变更条目动辄数千条。如何在有限的升级窗口期内，快速识别与自身业务真正相关的影响，一直是客户面临的实际挑战。

Release Assessment and Scope Dependency Tool（缩写为RASD）正是为此而设计的工具。它根据客户实际激活和使用的 Scope Item，自动过滤无关内容，将每次版本变更精简为一份个性化的影响清单，帮助客户聚焦真正需要处理的内容。

2026 年，SAP 持续增强 RASD 的功能，目标是进一步提升客户的升级体验，确保每次发版实现 Zero Day 1 Impact。本文将介绍其中一项重要新功能：Change History（变更历史记录）。

 

问题背景：Preliminary 到 RTC 之间的信息缺口

每个版本的 What's New 内容通常会在正式发布（RTC）前3～5 周以 Preliminary 版本提前上线。许多团队会在此阶段就开始评估升级影响——但在 RTC 正式发布前，这些内容仍可能持续更新。

此前，RASD 并没有追踪"上次评估完成后内容发生了哪些变化"的功能，团队往往需要在 RTC 临近时重新扫描全量内容，既耗时，又容易遗漏增量更新。

2026/2602 新功能：Change History

Change History 完整记录了某个版本的 What's New 内容从 Preliminary 到 RTC 各阶段的所有变更。用户无需每次从头审阅，只需关注上次评估之后新增或修改的条目。

这项功能的价值体现在三个方面：

  • 减少重复工作：已评估过的内容无需再次确认
  • 降低临期压力：RTC 发布前无需突击复查全量内容
  • 提升评估连贯性：从 Preliminary 到 RTC 的整个准备周期更加可控
如何使用

访问入口：登录 RASD 后，在 Complete What's New Viewer Tab 顶部可直接找到 Change History 的访问链接，如下图。

 

核心功能：Last Modified On 筛选器

每张 What's New Viewer 卡片内均提供 "Last Modified On" 日期筛选器，支持：

- 筛选指定日期之后更新的条目，仅查看增量变更
- 通过卡片内的直达链接，跳转至对应的 Change History 详情，逐条核查具体改动

 

使用建议：建议在完成每轮评估时记录日期，下次打开时直接以该日期为起点筛选，专注于真正新增的内容，避免在已确认条目上重复投入时间。

小结

Change History 是一项聚焦实际工作流的功能改进，有效填补了 RASD 在 Preliminary 阶段动态追踪上的空白。对于已将 RASD 纳入升级准备流程的团队而言，这项功能可以让整个评估节奏更加主动和精准。

RASD 的功能增强将贯穿 2026 年全年持续推出，后续更多更新请持续关注。

 

本文基于 Anjali Pathak 发布于 SAP Community 的原文编译整理，已获作者授权转载。如需了解 RASD 工具的基础功能，可参考 Angela Dai 此前发布的 中文版 RASD 2.0 介绍文章（请注意该文章距离发布已有一段时间，界面上的功能可能有微小调整升级）。

原文作者：Anjali Pathak
原文链接：Release Assessment and Scope Dependency for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: What is new in 2026

entry.title: Get Ready for the 2608 Upgrade of the SAP Cloud ERP Public Edition
entry.content:
Introduction

The time for the 2608 upgrade of the SAP Cloud ERP Public Edition is quickly approaching. I will highlight a few key dates and major changes for this upgrade.  The content is limited to the CBC Based 3 System Landscape.

 

Key Upgrade Dates

Here are the key dates related with this upgrade:

  • July 18 – 19, 2026: T-Tenant Upgrades to 2608 with HFC02.
  • August 1 – 2, 2026: Other tenants (Starter, Sandbox, Evaluation, etc.) upgrade to 2608 with HFC03; T-Tenant gets HFC03 as well for new feature preview.
  • August 15 – 16, 2026: D/P-Tenants Upgrade to 2608 with HFC03+; T-Tenant gets HFC03+ as well.

 

Changes in the Hot Fix Collection Application during the 2608 Upgrade

There are changes in applying the Hot Fix Collection (HFC) during the upgrade as shown below.

Changes in the Hot Fix Collection Application during 2608 Upgrade

 

Here are the changes:

  • 2602 Upgrade
    • D/P Tenant uses HFC04 to upgrade

 

  • 2608 Upgrade
    • Introduces a new HFC release called HFCxx+ with critical fixes, which is released one week after HFCxx.
    • T tenant receives HFC02 first, and HFC03 two weeks later.  This gives T tenant users two week in advance to preview the features introduced in HFC03 before D/P tenants are upgraded into HFC03+.
    • D/P tenants use HFC03+ to upgrade instead of HFC03; T tenant receives HFC03+ at the same weekend.

 

Changes in the Hot Fix Collection Pattern after the 2608 Upgrade

Besides HFC changes during the upgrade, the pattern of applying new HFC after the upgrade has been changed as well, as shown below.

Changes in the Hot Fix Collection Pattern after 2608 Upgrade

 

Here are the changes:

  • After 2602 Upgrade
    • T and D/P are applied the same HFC after upgrade, such as HFC08
    • Corrections-only HFC is released two weeks later and applied to all D/T/P tenants, such as HFC09
    • There is one week gap between HFCs
    • There is a Monthly Global infrastructure maintenance (dark blue blocks)

 

  • After 2608 Upgrade
    • T/other Tenants are applied with HFC after upgrade, such as HFC05
    • One week later, HFCxx+ is released with critical fixes.  D/P is only applied with HFCxx+ (skips HFCxx), such as HFC05+.
    • There are two weeks gap between HFCs, so that the system is more stable
    • There are three new monthly maintenance:
      • Standard Maintenance (dark green weekend for planned downtime activities)
      • Online Software Change for P (light green weekend for including infrastructure and offline software change to T/D)
      • Optional Maintenance (not shown but for unplanned & urgent activities)

 

To get the details about this forthcoming 2608 upgrade, please refer to the presentation listed in the reference SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, 3-System Landscape Upgrade & Maintenance Schedule (February 19, 2026).

 

References

  • My Blog: Understand Upgrading and Patching Processes of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • My Blog: FAQ on Upgrading SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • My Blog: Get Ready for 2602 Upgrade of the SAP Cloud ERP Public Edition
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, 3-System Landscape Upgrade & Maintenance Schedule (February 19, 2026)
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Software Lifecycle Management for Customers (September 3, 2025)

entry.title: SAP Connect Day for UX in Silicon Valley focused on AI strategy and tactics for implementation
entry.content:
SAP Connect Day for UX in Silicon Valley was a chance for customers to connect with each other, SAP experts, and selected partners to discuss how AI will improve the lives of SAP Cloud ERP users and the developers who build apps for SAP S/4HANA Cloud. The AppHaus in Palo Alto was filled with energy as we shared knowledge through a combination of lectures, hands-on sessions, and informal conversations. In this post, I’ll share some of the highlights and where you can learn more about the topics we discussed.

Strategy starts with where AI fits into SAP Cloud ERP

We showed demos of the latest user experience innovations in SAP Cloud ERP, also known as SAP S/4HANA Cloud. Starting with My Home, we explored enterprise search, card creation, smart situations, and AI-powered recommendations. In the SAP Fiori apps, we demonstrated proven productivity saving tools such as easy filter, easy fill, and smart summarization.

We briefly discussed SAP Fiori, including how it relates to design, development, and delivery of new apps in SAP Cloud ERP. This foundational session set the stage for the rest of the day, covering how SAP is bringing embedded AI to business users while keeping the SAP Design System and enterprise product standards at the center of the user experience.

Your data and queries are safe when using SAP AI.

There is often confusion when we talk about embedded AI and Joule. These two approaches are complementary. Joule provides a conversational interface to access SAP’s broad range of capabilities. It spans the entire autonomous suite.

Embedded AI provides targeted capabilities in both My Home and SAP Fiori elements apps within SAP Cloud ERP (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition). People can use their existing apps and business processes while benefitting from the productivity enhancements that AI provides. Any embedded AI capabilities can also be accessed through Joule.

Partners focused on bringing AI to customers on any platform

The sponsors demonstrated a deep knowledge of the SAP market with everyone sharing different ways of bringing AI capabilities to customers, regardless of whether they were running on-prem or in the cloud. Here is a list of the partner sessions:

  • SAP UX Eras tour: from Dynpro modifications to clean core – what we learned and how to do it right in 2026 (Dassian)
  • Disappearing Ink: Use Agents to Automatically Eliminate Manual Data Entry Across Your Business (cbs – Corporate Business Solutions)
  • From On-Premise to the Future: Enabling Agentic AI Across SAP Landscapes (eXXcellent solutions)
  • Have Your Agent Call My Agent: 20 Joule Agents, Claude, and Reimagining of the Fiori Experience (Mindset Consulting)

Each session offered a different angle on the same broad question every SAP customer is asking right now: how do I bring AI and agents into my SAP landscape in a way that delivers measurable value? The variety of approaches sparked great discussion and gave attendees plenty of reasons to visit the partners during the breaks to go into more detail than the 20-minute talks allowed.

Successful AI implementation is about productivity – the hands-on workshops demonstrated this

The afternoon sessions we split into two blocks: one on application development and the other on new features.

In the application development talk, we talked about SAP Fiori elements, SAP Fiori tools, and building apps using clean core principles. We followed that up with a hands-on exercise where participants could build an app using our latest tools and have the chance to try vibe coding to add and change features in the app without coding. They commented that this was much faster than traditional methods for developing new custom apps.

SAP Fiori elements makes it easy to build apps with enterprise features.

 

The next section was on Smart Helpers, a new way that business users can automate routine tasks in SAP Fiori apps in their SAP Cloud ERP system. We discussed the reasons why we built this capability and encouraged people to think about areas in their business where they could streamline recurring tasks in SAP. Then, they had a chance to try out the capabilities on a few simple scenarios. Smart Helpers operate according to these principles:

  • The user is in charge
  • IT remains in control
  • They are built on SAP Fiori apps
  • No coding is required

People shared several scenarios they would consider automating with Smart Helpers.

Tour of the Experience Center highlighted broader SAP capabilities

To provide a broader look at SAP’s Autonomous Suite we scheduled a tour of the Experience Center in the AppHaus. Visitors followed fictitious company, S.Tech, to see how they transform complex operational and supply chain challenges into opportunities. People saw different SAP solutions working together in a manufacturing scenario and enjoyed seeing the new demos.

 

What’s next for UX innovation in SAP Cloud ERP?

The day closed with a sneak preview of new functionality around Smart Helpers and a deeper dive into Joule Work. This triggered a rich discussion on Joule Work and how it fits into SAP’s portfolio. We addressed the following questions:

  • Does Joule Work replace SAP Fiori?
    No – the application experience and engagement layer experience are complementary. They will coexist for the foreseeable future. 
  • What is the future of SAP Fiori apps?
    SAP Fiori apps will coexist with Joule Work for the foreseeable future, just as SAP GUI transactions coexist with SAP Fiori apps. We are continuing to build SAP Fiori apps for SAP Cloud ERP and enhance the tools that we use and you can use to develop your own SAP Fiori apps. Tooling will include increasing amounts of AI to reduce the amount of coding required to create new apps.
  • When will we see all the cool embedded AI innovations in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition?
    We are working on moving most of the embedded AI capabilities to private cloud. Here is our current plan as of May 2026. As with any development plans or roadmaps, this is subject to change.
 

Public Beta

Public GA

Private GA

AI-assisted in My Home

   

AI-assisted Smart Personalization (Cards)

 

x

Planned

AI-assisted Smart Personalization (Apps and Tiles)

 

x

Planned

AI-assisted task automation (Smart Helpers)

x

 

Planned

AI-assisted smart solutions for situations in My Home

x

 

Planned

Smart summarization and broadcast via email for executives

x

 

Planned

    

AI-assisted beyond My Home

   

AI-assisted enterprise search

 

x

FPS02

AI-assisted easy filter

 

x

FPS02

AI-assisted smart summarization

 

x

FPS02

AI-assisted error explanation

 

x

Planned

AI-assisted easy fill

x

 

Planned

AI-assisted task automation (Smart Helpers)

x

 

Planned

 

Thanks to everyone who made the day a success

Thank you to our partners for their thoughtful sessions, to the behind-the-scenes team of developers, architects, product managers, and colleagues who built the features we showed, tested the systems, and created the hands-on exercises, and to the logistics team who made everything run smoothly.

Most of all, thank you to the attendees. Your enthusiasm, probing questions, and hallway conversations help us understand where you are in your SAP Fiori journey and how we can best prioritize what comes next.

 

Next steps

  • Register for the monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable call to meet other developers and architects who build SAP Fiori apps.
  • Subscribe to the SAP Fiori development newsletter, in which we share updates on SAP Fiori elements, SAP Fiori tools, SAPUI5, and SAP Build Code every other month.  
  • Join us at an another SAP Connect Day for UX in 2026 – we are finalizing locations, dates, and some new event formats.

On behalf of the SAP UX Foundation Team, Peter Spielvogel.

entry.title: Why does “Accounting Object Responsible” not Work for Asset-Based PR Approvals?
entry.content:
While working with Flexible Workflow for Purchase Requisitions, I came across a limitation that breaks an otherwise consistent and clean approval logic.

The Gap

In many setups, an asset is assigned to a cost center, and that cost center already has a defined User Responsible.

However, when creating a workflow for Account Assignment Category A (Asset) using the approver role “Accounting Object Responsible”, the workflow fails.

This creates an inconsistency:

  • For cost centers → works perfectly
  • For assets → ends in an error

The Missing Link

The expected logic seems straightforward:

PR → Asset → Cost Center → Responsible User → Approval

But currently, this derivation is not supported.

Why This Matters

Enabling this would:

✔️  Remove workflow errors and manual fixes

✔️  Ensure consistent approval logic across account assignments

✔️  Speed up PR approvals significantly

✔️  Improve accountability by addressing the right person

✔️  Support clean core by avoiding custom workarounds

The Ask 

Allow “Accounting Object Responsible” to be used for Account Assignment Category A (Asset), deriving the approver from the cost center assigned to the asset.

If you agree that this should be possible, 

👉  Please Vote to Idea # 375319 Approver "Accounting Object Responsible" should work for Account Assignment A on SAP Influence and help move this enhancement forward.

Kerisha Lawrence

Public Cloud | IAM, Procurement, Post Go-Live

https://www.linkedin.com/in/kerisha-lawrence
